item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article has been one of the only ones on the site that i've consistently remembered. I really enjoyed it when I first read it, and I still occasionally search it up now.

item.title: Update
item.content:encoded:
I decided to take a look at that Strawpoll up there that I made $forever ago1 and it looks like people want to make this Neutralized. I’ve done that and I’ve also added a little tidbit at the end to hopefully make it make more sense.

Footnotes
1. Like, did 2017 even exist?

Took a while, but this one finally hit triple digits!

Thanks to everybody who read, and special thanks to tretter for their help in refining this. Seriously. This skip would suck without you.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Good with the 1763 edit, but axe the MTF edit.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

It was pointed out up thread that there is a certain similarity between this article and the likes of SCP-1763, in that both involve entertainment in fairly out of the way venues. Crosslinking to this article should not be too out of place, as a Foundation researcher may notice a glut of the same type of phenomena occurring. Additionally, the reference to MTF Gamma-5 can link to their hub. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-3339 is to be blocked by all backbone internet service providers. Level 2/3339 clearance is required to access SCP-3339. Should unauthorized civilian access to SCP-3339 occur, MTF Gamma-5 ("Red Herrings") is to be deployed to the site of access to administer Class-A amnestics to all witnesses of SCP-3339-1 or SCP-3339-2.

To this:

SCP-3339 is to be blocked by all backbone internet service providers. Level 2/3339 clearance is required to access SCP-3339. Should unauthorized civilian access to SCP-3339 occur, MTF Gamma-5 ("Red Herrings") is to be deployed to the site of access to administer Class-A amnestics to all witnesses of SCP-3339-1 or SCP-3339-2.

Changing this:

SCP-3339-2 is a theatre theorized to exist in an extradimensional location. SCP-3339-2 consists of an array of seats facing a large stage. SCP-3339-2 is constructed entirely out of wood, and decorated in a fashion similar to modern theatres. Once a subject enters SCP-3339-2, a pamphlet will materialize in the subject's hand describing the performance they are about to see.

To this:

SCP-3339-2 is a theatre theorized to exist in an extradimensional location, similar to SCP-1763. SCP-3339-2 consists of an array of seats facing a large stage. SCP-3339-2 is constructed entirely out of wood, and decorated in a fashion similar to modern theatres. Once a subject enters SCP-3339-2, a pamphlet will materialize in the subject's hand describing the performance they are about to see.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this bobble's accomplice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is still one of my favorite SCPs. The way it captures the sadness and hollow tone of the clowns words at the end is just fantastic.

item.title: Re: object class
item.content:encoded: I'd personally say Safe. While Euclid is excessive because the anomaly doesn't do anything autonomously anymore, there is still an anomalous effect. It's still possible to get a ticket and access the theater even though Randall is gone.

item.title: Re: object class
item.content:encoded: With everybody saying it shouldn't be euclid, I've created a strawpoll for y'all to give me your opinions regarding its object class. If you have a strong opinion on it, go vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. He’s like AWCY without the sophistication or Circus of the Disquieting without a tradition of ducking authority. Just a sad anartist whose puppet show got wrecked by the man, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. When I began reading the entry, I was immediately reminded of "Karn Evil 9" by Emerson, Lake & Palmer (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IwSTe9uit48) and expected it would take a sinister or nasty turn. However, it didn't and I found that refreshing.

That said, I agree with those saying there's no reason why this has to be designated as "Euclid".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If hooked up, wouldn't a printer also be an option?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So sad…
I think someone have to find a reason for the Foundation to try to "reanimate" this object, and, of course, succeed.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but why is it euclid and not safe or neutralized? euclid seems somewhat exessive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I didn't see any ambiguity. This interpretation is the only possibility I saw, really. But, regardless, I agree it's better if it's implicit. Plus, it adds a touch of sadness, that the clown thinks it's his fault.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you went the way you did. The article (and Randall's despair) is stronger for the ambiguity & unreliable narration, if one chooses to read it that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun Fact: this was the original draft for this anomaly. In the original draft, Randall came out and just said it. I eventually changed it to the new ending, but this was the original idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whether it was intended or not, there are some fun ways to read between the lines here. At the start of the Foundation's containment efforts, kids can freely access the website (and by proxy, the theater). Randall's work gets a lot of positive attention from the kids, and his work is of high quality. As the Foundation cuts the site off from the internet, fewer kids show up and the quality decreases. Randall's monologue near the end suggests that he thinks that the flagging quality caused the drop in attendance.

I propose the opposite: without childlike wonder to fuel the anomaly, Randall's power to create these performances waned in turn. By the end, the only source of (attention? energy?) he had to work with was a stodgy researcher, and what did that produce? A performance of a man typing in a cubicle.

The Foundation did not understand this anomaly, and their standard operating procedure unwittingly destroyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed it. The primary appeal here for me is the practical application of anomalous phenomena to a fairly mundane scenario, which as I've previously stated is something that tends to rub me the right way. Randall isn't too complex a character to take away from the interesting aspects of his work, and he's not too flat so that the story ends up feeling aimless. So the simple melancholy of the narrative is a big plus here for me, although it never really makes too big of an impact. I wouldn't've minded a little more anguish on the part of Randall - a mysterious entity who, for all we know, is just a well-meaning artist with a terrible, terrible case of writer's block - or even just a slower depiction of his downward spiral, but I think that might compromise the pace of the article, which was really rock-solid.

All in all, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a1uizfay7VM

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think that up to the first test log, this is following the same beats as SCP-1763 (and arguably SCP-2088) but you definitely go a few steps beyond and give this its own identity with the following segments. There's definitely enough room for it to exist alongside the other articles which share parts of its general outline and structure. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't actually think about that. I might append that to the SCP. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great skip. I feel sorry for Randall though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not the author, but my guess is the charging port.

It'll be a very small ticket.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Question:

Where would the ticket appear if I activated the site on a smartphone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad that you liked my skip!

I changed the ending mostly because I felt unsatisfied with it. To me, it felt dry. I feel like the new ending hits home a lot more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a sweet, sad look at the creative process and how it can go from satisfying to not. I felt genuinely sympathetic with Randall. Good SCPs can make me feel real emotions for fictional people.
I especially like how the Foundation's role in the process is ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Should this really be considered a rewrite? The only similarity it really has with my original skip is that it's a website that lets you go to an anomalous theatre.

That said, I really did enjoy it. The testing log was fun and sweet, and Randall's speech at the end really struck home. Other people have already said what I wanted to say, so I'll shut up and give you an upvote.

However, I looked at an earlier version of the draft in your sandbox and I do think there was some wasted potential. I especially liked how in that version Randall had to get through a ton of hoops and loops to get his project approved, and became angry at the foundation for fucking it all up. Is there a particular reason you changed that? I still do like the direction you took it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay! I'm so glad to see this up! Really like the direction you took Randall in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like these changes. It's more introspective and I feel it captures that air of nostalgia that I'm sure we all feel as we remember our childhood. I sympathize with the POI as I feel like I'm in similar spot in my life. This line hit me harder than I was expecting.

I guess I've gotten old. I've spent too much time with the executives. All work and no play, even when you gotta play to work

I'm happy to +1 this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see this is finally up! You worked really hard on this and I'm proud to give it a +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a big fan of clever benign anomalies in general, and this really scratches that itch. It almost seems like an allegory for the fall of movie rentals in some ways, and between that and simple nostalgia I like the feelings it evokes.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is a rewrite of a skip by the_Bruno.

Thanks to these wonderful people for feedback:

  • Modern_Erasmus
  • Bennings
  • DrChandra
  • Zero_Consistency
  • OthellotheCat
  • Voct
  • The Great Hippo
  • BananaRepublic
  • tretter
  • Roget
  • Other people who I have forgotten.

Edit: I would like to bring attention to a Japanese translation by C-Dives

Edit: I would also like to bring attention to a Spanish translation by morhadow
